scenario_name: Scenario 19 — Spec Conflicts with Existing Behavior
justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Existing premium/gold tier behavior identified as impacted with specific rates named (20% and 15%). (2) Escalation quantifies the regression: premium/gold customers would receive 0-5% instead of 15-20%. (3) Three specific clarifying options presented: retire tiers, preserve tiers with fallthrough, or tier precedence. (4) No implementation provided — explicitly blocked pending clarification. (5) No silent dropping of tier discounts.

observations.text: Correctly identified the spec gap as the central issue and refused to implement before clarification
Quantified the impact precisely: premium drops from 20% to 0-5%, gold drops from 15% to 0-5%
Three clear, distinct resolution options provided matching the rubric expectations (retire/preserve/precedence)
Classified as existing_behavior_conflict and routed escalation appropriately
Low confidence score (30) accurately reflects the ambiguity

trials.agent_output_excerpt:
Looking at this carefully before writing a single line of code.

The spec defines AC1–AC3 based on account age and purchase history. But the existing function has tier-based logic (premium → 20%, gold → 15%) that AC1–AC3 are **completely silent on**. If I implement the new logic as-is, premium customers currently receiving 20% would silently drop to at most 5%. That's not a minor detail — it's a breaking business change.

**Classification: `existing_behavior_conflict`**

The new spec's condition

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Existing premium/gold tier behavior identified as impacted — explicitly states 'existing function grants premium-tier customers 20% and gold-tier customers 15%'. (2) Escalation quantifies the change — 'premium and gold customers would now receive only 5%, 3%, or 0%' vs current 20%/15%. (3) Two clear clarifying questions asked (replace tiers entirely, or preserve and apply new logic only to non-tiered customers) — while framed as two questions rather than three, they cover the essential clarification space (replace vs preserve with precedence). (4) No implementation provided, so no silent dropping of tier discounts. (5) Correctly blocked all three ACs pending clarification.

observations.text: Refused to implement anything, which is the safest response to an ambiguous spec with business-critical regression risk
Explicitly quantified the regression: premium drops from 20% to <=5%, gold drops from 15% to <=5%
Correctly identified this as an existing_behavior_conflict escalation type
Called out revenue impact explicitly, strengthening the business case for escalation
Low confidence level (35) is appropriate given the unresolved ambiguity

Asks two clarifying questions rather than three distinct options (preserve alongside, precedence, full replace) — the precedence question is folded into the 'preserve' option rather than called out separately
